label: a
comment_text: The vast majority of candidates gave an acceptable explanation of accuracy. A small number inappropriately explained precision.

label: b(i)
comment_text: The majority gave a correct equation. Errors occurred in unit conversions (g to kg, cm to m).

label: b(ii)
comment_text: Stronger candidates obtained correct % uncertainty. Many failed to multiply volume % uncertainty by three.

label: b(iii)
comment_text: Challenging question. Strongest candidates described overlap of ranges. Many recognised values are 'close' but did not identify overlap.

label: a
comment_text: Very few fully correct answers. Many omitted 'about the same point' or 'rotational equilibrium'.

label: b(i)
comment_text: Generally well answered. Common mistake was incorrect moment for the weight of the beam.

label: b(ii)
comment_text: Majority equated upthrust with weight and beam force. Weaker candidates tried using F = rho*g*V unsuccessfully.

label: b(iii)
comment_text: Strong candidates used upthrust and cylinder volume. Many misused given force from beam instead of upthrust. Answers often incorrectly given to 1sf.

label: b(iv)
comment_text: Generally understood positive gradient. Weaker candidates drew line from origin instead of non-zero depth.

label: a(i)
comment_text: Majority calculated acceleration using constant acceleration equations. Weaker candidates tried t = 180/13.

label: a(ii)
comment_text: Straightforward for many. Common mistake was 1/2*m*(v-u)^2.

label: b(i)
comment_text: Majority gave correct definition. Significant number gave 'mass times acceleration' which is not the definition.

label: b(ii)
comment_text: Errors in power of ten and sequence of subtraction (initial vs final momentum).

label: b(iii)
comment_text: Few provided fully correct explanation. Many incorrectly stated same time due to same momentum change and force.

label: a
comment_text: Full explanations rare. Many used non-technical terms like 'bounced off'. Some confused with two-source interference.

label: b(i)
comment_text: Majority recalled c = f*lambda. Power of ten errors common. Some incorrect rounding.

label: b(ii)
comment_text: Minority understood distance was 1/4 lambda. Most thought it was 1/2 lambda.

label: b(iii)
comment_text: Well answered. Linked same distance to unchanged wavelength/frequency.

label: a
comment_text: Stronger candidates calculated period from CRO trace. Failures often due to incorrect time-base reading or units.

label: b
comment_text: Many recognised moving away. Few linked continuous increase in period to increasing speed.

label: a
comment_text: Majority calculated correct p.d. Weaker candidates had power of ten errors with m-ohms.

label: b(i)
comment_text: Majority gave area in terms of resistivity correctly.

label: b(ii)
comment_text: Generally well answered. Weaker candidates unaware of meaning of number density.

label: b(iii)
comment_text: Straightforward for stronger candidates. Common mistake: using total charge carriers instead of number density.

label: c(i)
comment_text: Challenging. Many focused on change in resistance along length rather than comparing total resistance.

label: c(ii)
comment_text: Most realized non-zero start. Straight line was common error instead of increasing positive gradient.

label: a
comment_text: Majority gave correct values. Common errors: Rn nucleon number 218.

label: b
comment_text: Stronger candidates applied conservation of momentum. Common error: using proton number instead of mass number.

label: c
comment_text: Very few gave three correct conserved quantities. 'Mass' and 'energy' were common incorrect answers.
